data.title: Rigetti: Moonshot Physics Meets Market Math
data.description:
Intro

Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) sits at the intersection of frontier physics and public‑equity scrutiny. After a meteoric rally earlier in 2025, the stock now trades in the low‑double‑digits, giving the Berkeley‑based quantum pioneer a roughly $3.7 billion market value despite generating less than $10 million of annual revenue. That disconnect captures the essence of the story: investors are paying up today for breakthroughs that may not translate into durable cash flows for years.

Although quantum hype has cooled from the fever pitch of 2021, Rigetti’s recent demonstrations of error‑mitigation on its 84‑qubit Ankaa‑2 and the launch of the higher‑fidelity Ankaa‑3 show real scientific momentum. At the same time, first‑quarter financials underscored just how far commercialization still lags: revenue fell 52% year over year to $1.5 million while operating expenses climbed to $22.1 million.

Against that backdrop, this article adopts a neutral stance. The upside of a genuine technology inflection is balanced by execution risk, funding dilution, and a competitive landscape teeming with better‑capitalized rivals. Investors who can stomach binary outcomes may find optionality attractive, but patience remains the most valuable commodity in quantum.

Thesis

Rigetti threads a narrow path between moon‑shot ambition and harsh commercial reality. Its hybrid superconducting‑qubit architecture continues to notch benchmark improvements (median two‑qubit fidelity on Ankaa‑3 reached 98%, a 2.5x jump over the prior generation) suggesting the company can at least keep pace with tech titans pursuing rival platforms.

Yet, the broader industry is still pre‑product. Customers are experimenting, not operationalizing. Management’s model of shipping incrementally larger chips and exposing them through cloud integrators such as Amazon Braket hedges adoption uncertainty, but the strategy only pays if Rigetti can deliver error‑corrected performance before its cash pile runs thin.

Meanwhile, hyperscale incumbents like IBM and specialist peers IonQ and D‑Wave sprint down parallel paths, diluting any first‑mover edge. For investors, the stock therefore represents leveraged exposure to a transformative technology balanced by execution, funding, and adoption risks that could render outcomes binary. Until scientific milestones convert into a moat supported by sticky revenue, a hold rating is prudent.

Technology Roadmap

Rigetti’s R&D cadence has accelerated over the past 18 months. The company moved from its 84‑qubit Ankaa‑2 to Ankaa‑3 in under a year, folding in redesigned chip layouts that reduced crosstalk and bumped fidelity close to the 99% level many researchers view as the entry ticket to scalable error correction.

In collaboration with Riverlane, Rigetti recently demonstrated low‑latency feedback loops for quantum error correction on Ankaa‑2, a key proof‑of‑concept showing that its control stack can execute the fast cycles needed for logical qubits. While still far from production quality, the result narrows the gap with better‑funded rivals such as IBM, which targets a 1000‑plus qubit error‑corrected machine later this decade.

The next waypoint on Rigetti’s public roadmap calls for a >100‑qubit system with 99.5% median two‑qubit fidelity by year‑end 2025. If achieved, that milestone would push the architecture toward “utility‑scale” domains such as quantum chemistry. Failure, however, would hand valuable mindshare to trapped‑ion leader IonQ, which already claims commercial advantage for specific Monte‑Carlo workloads.

Commercial Traction

Revenue remains lumpy because most sales stem from milestone‑based government or research contracts. The UK National Quantum Computing Centre deal, which supplies a 24‑qubit system, exemplifies that mix: it provides predictable engineering deliverables but carries lower margins and limited follow‑on volume.

Rigetti’s cloud strategy offers broader reach. By exposing its processors through Amazon, Microsoft, and its own QCS platform, the firm can monetize compute cycles without costly direct‑sales teams. Yet, utilization remains thin; management has not disclosed meaningful usage metrics, and first‑quarter revenue per available qubit continues to drift lower as capacity outpaces demand.

Partnership announcements, such as a South Korean initiative with Norma to stand up an 84‑qubit cloud service, signal rising international interest but have yet to translate into backlog visibility. Until quantum advantage emerges for everyday workloads, Rigetti’s commercial flywheel will likely spin slowly.

Capital Position

Rigetti ended March 31 with $237.7 million of cash thanks to a February 2025 investment from Quanta Computer. On June 11 management opportunistically topped up liquidity through a $350 million “at‑the‑market” equity raise, lifting total cash to roughly $575 million and eliminating near‑term debt.

That runway supports at least two more processor generations under the current burn rate. Operating expenses of $22.1 million in the latest quarter imply annualized cash use of roughly $90 million, before capex and working‑capital swings. Absent revenue inflection, however, further dilution remains a material risk, particularly if macro conditions tighten risk capital for deep‑tech ventures.

Management has suggested that positive EBITDA could be possible once multiple utility‑class systems are sold annually, but even optimistic industry forecasts place that inflection no earlier than 2027. Investors therefore must weigh technological progress against ongoing dilution when sizing positions.

Financials

First‑quarter revenue dropped to $1.5 million from $3.1 million a year ago as milestone timing and fewer governmental projects weighed on top‑line comparisons. Gross margin remains volatile because most contracts bundle hardware and services; management did not provide an explicit figure, but commentary pointed to lower margins tied to the NQCC system.

Operating expenses rose 22% to $22.1 million, reflecting higher payroll and stock‑based compensation tied to expanded R&D and the Quanta partnership. Rigetti actually posted GAAP net income of $42.6 million, yet that headline profit was entirely due to a $62.1 million non‑cash gain from revaluing warrant and earn‑out liabilities; on an adjusted basis the company remained deep in the red.

Cash flow from operations was negative $18 million for the quarter after adjusting for the non‑cash derivatives gain, illustrating the persistent mismatch between scientific ambition and commercial traction. Full‑year 2024 revenue totaled only $10.6 million, down slightly year-on-year. Guidance for 2025 remains qualitative, emphasizing technical milestones rather than revenue targets.

Risks

Execution risk tops the list. Failure to deliver the >100‑qubit, 99.5%‑fidelity system by year‑end would undermine the claim that Rigetti can keep pace with larger rivals who enjoy broader fabs and bigger budgets.

Dilution is the second major concern. The June equity raise increased the share count above 300 million. If burn rates hold and revenue does not accelerate, another raise could arrive before 2027, pressuring per‑share economics.

Competitive intensity also looms large. IonQ (IONQ), with a $9.9 billion market cap and roughly $600 million of cash, is targeting $75–95 million of revenue this year (an order of magnitude above Rigetti) while D‑Wave promotes a gate‑model roadmap alongside its annealers. If those peers secure early commercial wins, Rigetti may struggle to remain on short‑lists despite technical parity.

Conclusion

At roughly 350x trailing‑twelve‑month sales, Rigetti’s equity clearly prices in an eventual payoff far beyond today’s lumpy contract revenue. IonQ trades at about 130x sales, while D‑Wave commands near 260x, leaving Rigetti the most expensive of the quantum pure plays despite lagging on commercial metrics.

Traditional multiples such as P/E, PEG, or EV/EBITDA are not meaningful because all three companies operate with negative earnings and cash burn. Investors therefore default to price‑to‑sales or enterprise‑value‑to‑research‑spend ratios, and on those measures Rigetti screens richly valued relative to peers with larger revenue bases and similar cash runways.

Quantum computing may yet transform entire industries, and Rigetti’s engineering progress keeps it in that race. Nevertheless, until the roadmap converts scientific milestones into recurring revenue and a moat, the shares look fully valued. The risk‑reward skew remains binary: quantum success could generate multibillion‑dollar upside, but missed technical or funding checkpoints could compress the multiple dramatically. On balance, a neutral/hold stance best reflects that asymmetry.

data.title: Valens Semiconductor: Undervalued Innovator With 200%+ Upside Potential In ADAS And Pro-AV
data.description:
Valens Semiconductor (NYSE: NYSE:VLN) has emerged as a noteworthy contender in the niche of high-speed connectivity semiconductors. Headquartered in Israel, the company has carved a reputation for delivering cutting-edge, proprietary DSP-based PHY layer technology that serves several demanding markets, including automotive, Pro-AV (professional audio-video), industrial machine vision, and medical technology. By leveraging its unique technology, Valens differentiates itself from larger competitors like Texas Instruments (TI) and Analog Devices (ADI).

Company Profile

Valens operates on a global scale, offering high-performance semiconductor chips renowned for their ability to transmit data over long distances with minimal latency and strong resistance to electrical noise. These capabilities are foundational to two key industry standards: HDBaseT and MIPI A-PHY, positioning the company as a crucial influencer in setting global connectivity benchmarks.

Technological Edge and Differentiation

The company’s key differentiator lies in its DSP-managed PHY layer, which allows for significantly better data integrity and immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI) when compared to traditional analog solutions. Highlights of their technological advantage include:

  • Real-time, DSP-based noise cancellation, enhancing performance in high-EMI environments such as vehicles and industrial settings.
  • Capability to transmit multi-gigabit data using inexpensive, unshielded cables, effectively lowering overall system costs.
  • Zero-latency, uncompressed data transmission, which is especially valuable for applications like ADAS in cars, high-resolution medical imaging, and professional AV setups.

Superiority of Valens’ PHY Technology

Valens utilizes sophisticated DSP-driven PHY technology to achieve high-bandwidth data transfer (several gigabits per second) over distances up to 100 meters using standard cabling. This greatly exceeds the performance capabilities of standard Ethernet in terms of both distance and reliability. Their focus on uncompressed video transmission, critical for time-sensitive applications, offers a marked advantage. While some high-end AV systems might accept minimal compression, Valens’ no-compromise approach ensures optimal data fidelity.

In terms of electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), Valens’ chips consistently outperform automotive Ethernet counterparts, making them particularly attractive in the automotive sector. The company has labeled its victories in the automotive and industrial sectors as "knockout" wins, citing their success in stringent OEM testing as evidence of their technological leadership.

Robust DSP-Managed PHY Layer

The advanced PHY layer embedded in Valens chips enables gigabit-scale data transmission across affordable cables stretching as far as 100 meters (approximately 328 feet), far surpassing industry norms. The proprietary noise cancellation method demonstrates over 20 times greater EMI resilience than legacy analog approaches. Demonstrations involving A-PHY technology confirm that Valens maintains signal integrity even under extreme EMI conditions, where other systems fail—a crucial factor in safety-critical automotive applications.

Financial Health and Outlook

Valens is on a defined path of expansion, aiming to grow revenue from roughly $57 million in 2024 to between $220 million and $300 million by 2029. The company anticipates reaching EBITDA profitability once revenue surpasses the $110 million to $120 million mark. Here is a segment-wise financial breakdown:

  • Pro-AV: Expected to grow from $32 million (2024) to around $90–$100 million (2029), with gross margins between 65% and 75%.
  • Automotive: Forecasted to expand from $21 million (2024) to $65–$110 million (2029), with lower margins of roughly 35%, due to competitive pricing and specific market dynamics.
  • Industrial Machine Vision: Poised to grow from $1 million (2024) to $35–$50 million (2029), with estimated gross margins in the 55% to 65% range.
  • Medical: Still in the early stages, this segment has a total addressable market (TAM) of approximately $625 million, contingent upon widespread adoption of single-use endoscopes.

Valens experienced a peak in revenue at $91 million in 2022 after growing from $60 million in 2019. Revenue is expected to temporarily dip to about $57 million in 2024, attributed to post-COVID inventory normalization and customer budget adjustments.

Gross Margin Insights

Valens’ gross margins vary significantly across sectors: approximately 70% in Pro-AV and about 35% in automotive. The company anticipates maintaining a blended gross margin around 60% upon scaling, with operating expenses hovering near $80 million annually.

Revenue Evolution and Strategic Distribution

The company’s revenue mix is gradually diversifying:

  • In 2024: Pro-AV (~60%) and Automotive (~40%) dominate.
  • By 2029: Expected distribution is 45% Pro-AV, 32% Automotive, 18% Industrial Vision, and 2% from other sources. Medical remains a latent, long-term growth opportunity.

Competitive Landscape Review

Valens faces its primary competition in automotive connectivity from established players like TI and ADI. However, several differentiators put Valens in a strong position:

  • Technology Superiority: The DSP-managed PHY technology outperforms analog alternatives in both resilience and simplicity.
  • Cost Advantage: Valens' approach with unshielded cables results in significantly reduced system costs.
  • Standards Leadership: Unlike competitors, Valens plays a central role in shaping connectivity standards, particularly through MIPI A-PHY.

Key Growth Catalysts

Multiple forces are driving Valens' revenue outlook:

  • Automotive: Secured design wins with top OEMs including Mercedes-Benz, plus three additional European OEMs, and a strategic partnership with Mobileye to capitalize on ADAS opportunities.
  • Pro-AV Expansion: Increasing adoption of the VS6320 chipset, particularly for video conferencing in hybrid workplaces.
  • Industrial Vision: Rising demand driven by automation and AI-enhanced inspection workflows.
  • Strategic Partnerships: Collaborations with Acroname and ESWIN Computing are helping Valens deepen its market reach.

Segment-Specific Revenue Outlook (Source)

  • Pro-AV: Projected revenue of $90–100 million by 2029, driven by continued uptake of the VS6320 chip.
  • Industrial Machine Vision: Expected to expand from under $1 million to $35–50 million.
  • Automotive: Revenue predicted to reach $65–110 million, particularly as ADAS solutions scale in 2026 and beyond.

Opportunities in Each Market

  • Pro-AV: HDBaseT standard leadership and innovations like the VS6320 (USB 3.2 extension) position Valens well.
  • Automotive: Design wins and ADAS deployments with major OEMs support the $65–110 million forecast.
  • Industrial Machine Vision: The VA7000 chip addresses high-bandwidth, high-reliability needs for automation.
  • Medical: The single-use endoscopy market holds future promise, contingent on regulatory and clinical uptake.

Segment Financial Summary

Design Win Timelines

The typical cycle from evaluation to revenue generation:

Evaluation Phase:

  • Pro-AV: 6 months
  • Industrial: 6-12 months
  • Automotive: 12-18 months

Customer Development Phase:

  • Pro-AV: 6-9 months
  • Industrial: 9-12 months
  • Automotive: 24-30 months

Mass Production Phase:

  • Pro-AV: 3-5 years
  • Industrial: 5-7 years
  • Automotive: 5-7 years

Supply Chain Considerations

Valens operates in the high-end segment, which has shielded it from many supply chain disruptions. The company has never delayed customer production due to chip shortages, a testament to its reliability. That said, its heavy reliance on a few foundries—notably TSMC and Samsung—poses concentration risk. Chip redesigns for alternate foundries can take six months to two years, depending on whether the chip is digital or analog.

Valuation Methodologies and Target Price

Using a multipronged approach to valuation:

  • DCF Model: Utilized a 9% WACC, showing robust upside potential under both base and optimistic cases.
  • EV/EBITDA Model: Applied multiples in the 17x–23x range, yielding strong valuations.
  • P/E Analysis: Factored in EPS estimates and equity dilution, with P/E ratios from 24x to 28x.

Price Targets:

  • Base Case: $5.10/share (137% upside)
  • Best Case: $7.05/share (228% upside)

Long-Term Strategic Position and Risk Factors

Valens is well-positioned to tap into long-term trends, particularly in medical imaging and automotive ADAS. However, several risks persist:

  • Lengthy automotive design cycles
  • Supply chain concentration
  • Intense competition from well-established firms
  • Tariff and geopolitical tensions

Capital Strategy and Shareholder Alignment

The $25 million share repurchase program launched by Valens reflects management’s confidence in the company’s intrinsic value and future prospects. This signals prudent capital deployment and alignment with shareholder interests.

Conclusion

Valens Semiconductor presents a compelling case for investors looking to diversify within the semiconductor sector. With a clear technological edge, involvement in key industry standards, and a path toward scalable, diversified growth, Valens stands out as a high-potential investment opportunity. The company’s traction across multiple high-growth markets, paired with disciplined execution and thoughtful capital management, reinforces its long-term value proposition.

data.title: Equinox Gold: Calibre Mining Acquisition Looks Like A Bargain Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Equinox Gold (NYSE:EQX) is undergoing a transformation. Historically, Equinox was characterised by many smaller, higher cost mines in riskier jurisdictions, bringing inefficiency and lack of scale. Things are beginning to change though. The opening of the large low-cost Greenstone mine in Canada and the acquisition of Calibre Mining (OTCQX:CXBMF), adding the promising Valentine project, marks a significant shift in the portfolio both in quality and output.

Despite a 28% rise in the share price so far this year following the gold price higher, the stock has lagged the wider VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). With vast reserves, production growth from Greenstone and the Calibre deal, and with the gold price continuing to head higher, I believe investors are underestimating Equinox’s potential.

In this article, I want to explore Equinox’s latest results, look in more detail at the Calibre deal and explain why I believe Equinox shares are a buy.

Q1 Earnings

Equinox Gold recently reported its Q1-25 earnings, with quarterly production of 145,300 ounces of gold, a 30% increase in production compared to Q1-24. The increase was primarily driven by the production from the company’s Greenstone mine which was not operation in Q1 last year. The production increase was, however, partially offset by lower production at the Mesquite and Aurizona mines.

Starting at the Mesquite Gold Mine in California, production dropped 44% to 12,271 ounces from 22,025 ounces in Q1-24. This was due to a focus on the Ginger pit, which involved significant stripping activity. The ore body was reached near the end of the quarter and is now being stacked on the leach pad which should mean improved production later this year. The Aurizona mine in Brazil saw a 35% decline to 15,555 ounces from 23,857 ounces, driven by 26% lower tonnes processed (594 kt vs. 803 kt) and 16% lower grades (0.87 g/t vs. 1.04 g/t). Moving on to the RDM mine, there was a small dip in production of 222 ounces, leaving production 10,710 ounces, as increased ore volumes were offset by lower grades (0.55 g/t vs. 0.62 g/t).

On the other hand, production growth was experienced at the Bahia Complex in Brazil, which saw production of 27,565 ounces (+5%), on the back of more tonnes processed and a slight improvement in grades (1.32 g/t vs. 1.19 g/t). The final existing mine is the Los Filos mine in Mexico which experienced a 32% production increase to 31,518 ounces as there was a drawdown in heap leach inventory, despite lower ore mined. Operations have, however, now been suspended indefinitely as, Equinox failed to reach a long-term agreement with one of the three communities that host Los Filos, with them requesting negotiations independent of the other two communities. The existing agreement expired on 31st March, with production now suspended indefinitely.

The main driver of production growth was not from any existing mine, but the new Greenstone mine in Ontario, Canada. Not in production in Q1 last year, the mine is still ramping up production with 44,449 ounces produced in Q1-25. This was, however, a 16% dip from Q4-24 as grades (1.06 g/t vs. 1.26 g/t) decreased due to mine sequencing and reduced leach tank availability due to planned modifications. Whilst the ramp-up has been slow, Greenstone is expected to transform Equinox’s production profile with 2025 production guidance of 300,000 to 350,000 ounces at an AISC of $1,045–$1,145 per ounce, with output expected to grow quarterly as higher-grade areas are accessed. The mine is on track to reach its full production target of 400,000 ounces annually in H2-25, supported by ongoing increased mining rates and mill throughput.

With production growth of 30% and Equinox benefitting from a record realized gold price of $2,858 (+38 YoY), revenue saw a 76% increase to $423.7 million.

Equinox reported a cash flow from operations of $73.3 million, a significant improvement from the $47.7 million in the same quarter last year. This translated into a free cash flow of $57.7 million. On the balance sheet side, Equinox had $172.9 million in cash and cash equivalents at quarter end, and a total debt of $1,499 million. With $500.5 million in EBITDA in the trailing 12 months, this places Equinox on a net debt to EBITDA rating of just under 3. Whilst this suggests debt is at a fairly elevated level and is costing Equinox around $100 million a year in interest expenses, it is important to remember that Equinox has just completed a significant capital investment project with the building of the Greenstone mine. With Greenstone now in production, I anticipate debt levels to fall quickly as production ramps up.

Continuing on to costs and margins, Equinox reported a first quarter all in sustaining cost [AISC] of $2,075 per ounce of gold, an increase from $1,950 per ounce a year earlier. Whilst this is amongst the highest AISC in the sector, the overall AISC for 2025 is set to come in at $1,800 to $1,900 per ounce. Once Greenstone has fully ramped up, this is set to fall, with Greenstone achieving an AISC of $1,095 at the midpoint of guidance.

Looking ahead, Equinox updated its 2025 guidance following its pending acquisition of Calibre Mining. The combined firm is expected to produce 785,000 to 915,000 ounces of gold in 2025, at an AISC of $1,800 to $1,900 per ounce. This compares to Equinox’s original guidance of 635,000 to 750,000 ounces at an AISC of $1,455 to $1,550 per ounce and reflects the slower than anticipated ramp-up at Greenstone.

Overall, this translated into a net loss of $75.5 million, compared to a net loss of $42.8 million last year. On an adjusted basis this was a loss of $36.6 million and equivalent to a loss per share of $0.17. Overall, Equinox is beginning to show signs of improvement. High metal prices provided a nice boost to revenues, and with Greenstone ramping up the company looks like its heading into a stronger position. With the Calibre Mining acquisition, we can expect even more growth in the future, with the Valentine mine set to be a new jewel in Equinox’s crown.

Acquisition of Calibre Mining

The biggest news of the year so far is the announcement of Equinox’s acquisition of Calibre Mining for 0.35 Equinox shares per Calibre share, valuing the transaction at $1.95 billion at the current share price.

This acquisition adds three producing mines, the Pan Mine in Nevada, and the Limon Mine Complex and La Libertad Mine Complex in Nicaragua. In 2024, these mines achieved total production of 242,487 ounces [35koz from Nevada mines, 207koz from Nicaraguan mines], at an AISC of $1,583 roughly in line with the industry average.

In addition, and perhaps most importantly, this deal means Equinox will become the owner of the Valentine Mine, which is set to begin production in Q3 2025. Located in Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada, Calibre Mining obtained the project through its takeover Marathon gold. The December 2022 Feasibility Study suggested a mine life of 14.3 years, with average annual production of 195,000 ounces of gold. The mine has 2.7 million ounces of proven and probable reserves at a grade of 1.62 g/t and almost 4 million ounces of measured and indicated reserves. AISC was estimated to be $1,007 per ounce at the time of the feasibility study, though this will likely be higher given the mining cost inflation in the period. This should, however, still come in below Calibre’s existing AISC of $1,583 an ounce.

With recent drilling results from the Frank zone showing intercepts like 2.43 g/t Au over 172.8 metres, there is clear potential for reserves and mine life to be extended further. Valentine is set to be a transformative mine, operating in a tier-one mining jurisdiction, with a good mine life, strong production profile, and with potential upside from further exploration. With the mining industry famous for a history of bad capital allocations, the question is, did Equinox get Calibre for a good price?

When Valentine is fully operational this will add 195koz to Calibre’s existing assets which produce 255koz a year, for a total annual production of 450koz. At 0.35 Equinox shares per Calibre share, this values the transaction at $1.95 billion at the current share price. With the existing mines running at an AISC of around $1,550 an ounce, I will use this as my AISC for valuing the Calibre acquisition. This is likely to be an overestimated AISC with Valentine likely to come in at a lower AISC, though probably not as low as the 2022 feasibility study due to inflation.

With near zero interest costs at Calibre, and Equinox shares currently trading at $6.44, my calculations suggest that at the current gold price this deal is highly accretive to Equinox. Equinox currently trades at a trailing free cash flow multiple of 7.17x, and the wider sector at a median of 9.59x trailing free cash flow. My calculations suggest that when Valentine is fully ramped up, if the gold price is $3,400 per ounce, Equinox is getting Calibre gold on a 3.31x multiple. Even if the gold price falls back to $2,600 an ounce, this deal still looks accretive, and this is with my calculations using an AISC that is probably on the high side for the Valentine mine. Equinox bought Calibre for a song.

So where does this leave Equinox. When Greenstone [400koz/yr] and Valentine [195koz/yr] are fully ramped up and combined with existing production from Equinox’s existing mines [400koz/yr] and Calibre [255koz/yr], this will give Equinox annual production of over 1.2 million ounces per year.

Equinox will have transitioned from a miner reliant on smaller, higher cost mines to an over one-million-ounce producer, with an improved risk profile with over 50% of production from tier one jurisdictions, at a reduced AISC. Overall, this seems like a great deal for Calibre and when Greenstone and Valentine are fully operational, Equinox should be printing money.

Valuation

With precious metal miner’s earnings heavily reliant on the price of the underlying commodity they mine, an exact value for the miner will vary depending on the metal's price. In times of higher precious metal prices, all miners stand to benefit. However, each miner has a different starting value and operations that will impact future returns. As such, I believe one way to get the relative value of Equinox Gold is in comparison to its precious metal mining peers such as Agnico Eagle (AEM), New Gold (NGD), Barrick Mining (B), Kinross Gold (KGC), IAMGOLD (IAG), Endeavor Gold (OTCQX:EDVMF), Goldfields (GFI) and Lundin Mining (OTCQX:LUGDF). Whilst all of these have significant precious metal operations, they all have large differences in cash and debt levels, so a direct comparison of earnings multiples will not suffice, and I instead use an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple.

Looking at the above, we can see that Equinox Gold trades at an EV to EBITDA multiple of 4.15, well below its peers. This is in spite of soon having two large, lower cost mines in a tier one jurisdiction making up over 50% of production. Whilst Equinox certainly doesn’t deserve to trade at the same levels as Agnico Eagle (AEM) given Agnico’s mine portfolio and history of execution, trading well below its peers seems to be undeserved given the ongoing transformation at Equinox and what I believe is a highly accreditive takeover of Calibre Mining.

To determine a price value for Equinox, I estimate the free cash flow and apply a multiple to this. I take the end of 2026 scenario where both Greenstone and Valentine should hopefully be fully ramped up giving running annual production of around 1.2 million ounces of gold. I assume a tax rate of 30% and AISC of $1,550 per ounce for the 450koz from Valentine and Calibre’s existing mines, an AISC of $1,300 per ounce for 400koz of Greenstone production, and an AISC of $1,750 per ounce from Equinox’s 400koz production from other mines. This gives an overall weighted average AISC of $1,600 per ounce, though this is likely to be an overestimation on my part.

Assuming a gold price of $3,000 an ounce, roughly 10% below current precious metal prices, we get a free cash flow of $1.05 billion at the end of 2026. I apply a free cash flow multiplier of 7.25, the average of the mining sector [8.32] and Equinox’s multiple [6.18], which gives a market cap of $7.61 billion. With around 512 million fully diluted shares when including Equinox’s convertible debt and the issuance of 297 million shares for the acquisition of Calibre, this gives Equinox a total fully diluted share count of around 810 million shares. With a market cap of $7.61 billion and 810 million shares outstanding, this gives a target price of $9.38 per share, 46% higher than today's current share price. This again is likely to be an underestimate as I have likely overestimated the AISC for Equinox, and the gold price currently trades above the $3,000 per ounce price I used. As such, I believe Equinox Gold is a buy at this price.

As with any investment, investing in Equinox Gold is not without its risk. With the company’s revenue and earnings dependence on the price of gold, I believe this is the primary risk. Any significant reduction in the price of gold will significantly reduce earnings and drive the share price lower. A further important risk is the mixed jurisdictional profile of their operations. Whilst Canada may present little risk as a tier one jurisdictions, the other locations of operations bring the risk of sudden regulatory changes and political uncertainty, which could negatively impact operations; just look at Equinox’s Los Filos mine in Mexico. In addition, there poses the risk of further share dilution. Whilst the Calibre Mine acquisition may be accretive, Equinox has a history of increasing the share count, though hopefully this risk will reduce in the future given Greenstone is now cash generating.

Conclusion

Equinox Gold’s acquisition of Calibre Mining marks a key addition to its portfolio. Equinox obtains a high quality soon to be producing mine in the Valentine project, in addition to three currently producing mines. This comes at what appears to be a great price of 0.35 Equinox shares per Calibre share, which by my calculations suggests this deal is highly accretive.

With production continuing to ramp up at Greenstone, Equinox is well on its way to transitioning from being reliant on smaller, higher cost mines in weaker jurisdictions, to operating multiple high-quality mines in a tier one jurisdiction.

With the gold price remaining near record levels, and the accretive acquisition of Calibre Mining, Equinox Mining is well placed for the future. With my calculations suggesting Equinox Mining is undervalued at its current price, I assign a buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NuScale Power Attracts Data Center Giants With Deployable SMR Technology
data.description:
NuScale Power (SMR) is at a pivotal moment. In May 2025, it became the first company ever to receive approval from the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission for a commercially deployable SMR design with 77 megawatts of electrical capacity. This came 2 months earlier than expected, marking not only a technical milestone but also a strategic weapon in a market increasingly eager for clean and reliable energy. At the same time, the White House signaled a series of new policies that are set to quadruple the size of the US nuclear power industry by 2050. As a result, stocks like NuScale have skyrocketed, jumping more than 17% in a single week.

But there’s a deeper story behind the news. NuScale is the only publicly traded company in the world that offers a fully approved, turnkey SMR solution. The commercial applicability, combined with a strong cash position, a global partner network and growing interest from AI data centers, gives NuScale a unique starting position. The question is whether investors (despite the speculative valuation) should get in now or rather watch from the sidelines. My belief is that the stock offers a rare opportunity today for those who are willing to look through the fog of uncertainty.

Political Momentum, Data-driven Demand and A Core Of Certainty

NuScale's strategic timing could hardly be better. While many nuclear projects fail due to licensing procedures and investment fears, NuScale has already received the official stamp of approval. The 77 MWe reactor received NRC approval in May 2025 and can therefore be legally used for construction and operation. Competitors such as GE-Hitachi, Oklo, Rolls-Royce or EDF are still in the early development phases. Even the Darlington project in Canada, led by GE-Hitachi, is not aiming for commissioning until around 2029. The American market is in a hurry.

This has everything to do with the energy demand from hyperscalers. AI and data centers are putting a heavy burden on the grid. In the US, electricity consumption by data centers is growing by double digits every year. The big players (Amazon, Google, Microsoft) are desperately looking for 24/7 carbon-free energy. Solar energy is not constant, wind is erratic, hydropower is geographically limited. Nuclear is suddenly no longer a relic of the Cold War but a crucial building block for AI.

NuScale knows this and is acting accordingly. The company is in advanced discussions with 5 hyperscalers, all under confidentiality. Several parties have already sent their teams to South Korea to visit Doosan’s factory, where NuScale’s modules are produced. These visits are not without obligation. Term sheets have been exchanged. According to CEO John Hopkins, the first firm customer order is a matter of months, not years.

Internationally, interest is also concrete. The RoPower project in Romania (led by NuScale and Fluor) is in phase 2 of the design process. The goal: a final investment decision by Q1 2026. The site is already ready for construction, the money largely pledged. If this goes ahead, it will mark the first SMR project in Europe.

What sets all these initiatives apart is that they are more than letters of intent. While competitors sign MOUs that rarely lead to revenue, NuScale is already warming up. The company earns engineering and licensing fees, grows with control over its supply chain and develops technologies for industrial applications such as hydrogen production and desalination.

Financially Conservative, Operationally Accelerated

Perhaps most attractive to NuScale is its combination of risk appetite and financial discipline. In the first quarter of 2025, the company generated $13.4 million in revenue, nearly 10x times the year-ago level. That growth comes from engineering work for RoPower but also from preparatory services for new customers. At the same time, expenses remained manageable: $42.3 million in OPEX, well below the 2023 quarterly average.

The cash position is robust. Thanks to an at-the-market equity program, NuScale now has $521 million in cash and short-term investments. There is no debt. Even without new deals, the company can move forward for at least 2 years. CFO Robert Hamady put it bluntly: "We plan based on conservative scenarios. Not on hope, but on runway."

That runway is necessary. The supply chain must be paid up front, suppliers like Doosan and Framatome require commitment. NuScale is already investing in long-lead items like reactor vessels and control systems. According to Hopkins, 12 modules are already in production, in anticipation of the first commercial deal. That is unusual for a pre-revenue energy company but crucial here.

It means that NuScale will soon be able to deliver quickly, while competitors still have to build factories. The business model (in collaboration with ENTRA1 Energy) provides a carefree turnkey solution: financing, development, operation. ENTRA1 is NuScale's exclusive partner and offers customers flexibility and speed.

Speculative Valuation or Early Opportunity?

Still: NuScale is not cheap. The market value flirts with several billions, while the annual revenue will probably remain below $60 million. The P/S multiple is above 50x. Traditional valuation models run wild here. There is no profit, no dividends, no EBITDA positivity in sight.

But that is not the point. Whoever buys NuScale buys a vision. The market price does not reflect the present but the chance that NuScale will grow into the supplier of modular nuclear energy in the 2030s.

That chance is not imaginary. Governments worldwide are putting nuclear energy back on the map. Coal-fired power stations are being phased out. Data centers are popping up like mushrooms. And NuScale is the only party with something that is really allowed to be built.

There are hardly any comparable companies. Oklo is a micro-SMR company with a lower market cap but also less concrete technology. GE-Hitachi is part of a conglomerate. Rolls-Royce and EDF have SMR plans but their designs are far from approved. In this respect, NuScale is a rare pure play.

So the premium investors are paying is partly justified. But the margin for error is small. If NuScale doesn’t close a deal by 2025, sentiment will quickly turn. A yes without an order means dilution, delays, and probably a sharp price correction.

Realistic Optimism

However, there are risks such as delays in the Romanian project. Political changes in Washington or Brussels. Delivery problems at Doosan. A watered-down ATM issuance. But those risks aren’t unique to NuScale, they apply to every player in this space. The difference is that NuScale has been ahead for years and 2025 is the moment when that lead can finally be cashed in. That doesn’t make this a hype but a calculated gamble. And maybe even a little more than that. A rare instance where technology, policy, market demand, and capital come together.

Worth The Risk

However, the stock does offer something interesting for investors with an investment horizon of 5 to 10 years. NuScale has proven technology, credible partners, and money in the bank. It is poised to be the first to deliver commercial-scale SMR.

That makes it an asymmetric investment. If everything goes wrong (no deals, no adoption) the stock could be cut in half. If it succeeds, and the first orders come in, a market worth hundreds of billions opens up. Today’s price could turn out to be a bargain.

My view is a “cautious buy” for investors who understand the risk and are willing to temporarily look past uncertainty. The stock is speculative but not irrational. Anyone who believes that nuclear is making a comeback, that AI requires a structural energy boost, and that scalable technology is needed to make that possible, can hardly ignore NuScale.

data.title: Chime Financial: A Strong Public Debut
data.description:
Shares of Chime Financial (NASDAQ:CHYM) have seen a solid public debut, although accompanied by some real volatility in the first days of trading. This clearly shows strong demand for the offering.

While strong operating momentum is to be applauded, shares trade at demanding valuation multiples, which makes me cautious as I wonder about the long term strength and longevity of the business model.

Unlock Financial Progress To Unite Everyday People

Founded back in 2012, Chime Financial aims to address the most critical financial needs of everyday Americans under the belief that the traditional banking system does not serve the everyday American.

The idea was for the business to develop a revolutionized business model which brings profits to members, instead of profiting from members. This came as many people are stuck in banking relationships which are penalizing to members. With many consumers holding very limited deposits in their accounts, banks have resorted to fee models. The company specifically targets Americans making $100k, or less a year, making up some 75% of the population.

The company aimed to tackle this unfair situation by focusing on payment-driven business models to create better alignment. Services include spending, liquidity, credit building, savings, community, support & safety. By now, some 8.6 million consumers have relations with Chime, with the company having primary bank account relationships with the majority of the member base.

Two-thirds of its members rely on Chime as the primary financial relationship. Through its platform, members deposit their paychecks and conduct every day spent, with active members on average doing more than 50 transactions through the platform per month, many of which are non-discretionary spending items.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Chime aimed to sell 32 million shares in a preliminary price range between $24 and $26 per share, with final pricing set at $27 per share. With nearly 26 million shares sold by the business itself, gross proceeds from the offering came in at $702 million, excluding the over-allotment option.

With over 364 million shares outstanding, equity of the business is valued at $9.8 billion at the offer price. This actually includes a roughly $1 billion net cash position on a pro forma basis, suggesting that operating assets are valued at around $8.8 billion at the offer price.

The company generated $1.01 billion in sales in 2022 on which a large operating loss of $479 million was reported. Revenues rose by 27% to $1.28 billion in 2023, with operating losses reported at $236 million. Revenues rose by another 30% to $1.67 billion in 2023 as operating losses narrowed down further to $62 million. The revenue take of the business in relation to underlying spent came in at just over 1%, with purchase volume for 2024 surpassing $115 billion.

Revenue growth continued, and in fact accelerated, in the first quarter of 2025. First quarter sales of nearly $519 million were up more than 32% on the year before. The company even managed to post operating profits of $9 million on a GAAP basis, comparing to actually a $5 million profit in the first quarter of last year already. Note that the growth in sales far supersedes the growth in the purchase volume, which only rose by 18% to $34.5 billion. Part of the discrepancy comes as the company does not rely entirely on payment volumes on which it captures a fee, but it offers other services as well.

Revenues trend just over $2 billion per annum, while growth comes in at >30% and the business is modestly profitable here. Given an $8.8 billion operating asset valuation at the offer price, the business is valued at just over 4 times sales, but comes amidst lack of real earnings. Trading at $35 in the days after the offering, the equity valuation of the firm comes in at $12.7 billion, which if we subtract cash holdings, makes for an enterprise valuation just shy of $12 billion.

This values the business at nearly 6 times sales, and a non-meaningful earnings multiple. Furthermore, investors have seen huge volatility in the first days of trading, as shares briefly traded in the mid-forties, at which point in time the valuation stood some three and a half billion higher.

Concluding Thoughts

Other than the valuation risks, there are some key risks in the business model which includes growth in the active member base, relationships with banking partners, including The Bancorp Bank master service agreement, as well as a dual class share arrangement.

Further risks include potential changes in rules and regulation regarding interchange fees and other related fees. Other risks include competition from various angles of course, including traditional banks, as well as financial technology firms like Affirm, Klarna and PayPal

The biggest risk for me is the fact that the company is facing disruption as some kind of middle men here, with the company not having own bank licenses, but relying on bank partners to be able to do its business. Given all this, I am very cautious, also given the valuations attached to the business here, and heightened expectations.

That said, both sales momentum and operating leverage is impressive enough to keep a close eye on the business, looking for further clues about growth and operating leverage in the quarters to come, but I need real conviction and/or other share price levels before seeing appeal given this situation here.

data.title: Adobe: Risks Of Being Upended By AI Rivals Are Getting Real (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Adobe: The Slow Bleed Continues

As a software and tech investor, I've often thought some companies like Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) could somehow rummage their way out of the Generative AI morass, reinventing themselves. Or they could somehow innovate out of the threat emanating from the AI-first companies seeking to snag market share from Adobe. While investors in the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) have generally benefited from the surge in shares like Microsoft (MSFT) and Palantir (PLTR), ADBE has "conspired" to drag down the performance of IGV, as the company behind the suite of creative content software tools is arguably facing the biggest disruption threat in its recent history.

Adobe has already made clear of such risks from these new AI upstarts in its regulatory filings. It also highlighted the legal ramifications even as it further incorporates the use of Gen AI in its AI tools (such as in Firefly). The recent lawsuit that Disney (DIS) and Universal (CMCSA) ignited against Midjourney is a reminder that Adobe must be careful in wading through the minefields of copyrighted content, while contending with competitive challenges from companies who may not necessarily share a similar philosophy.

Despite that, AI companies like Midjourney have also seen their revenue surge over the past two years, while Adobe has arguably struggled to replicate a similar revenue growth cadence. $250M+ in AI ARR? Seriously? It doesn't even move the needle for Adobe if we consider its overall revenue base of more than $23.5B in forecasted revenue for FY2025.

In other words, Adobe doesn't seem to have gained enough AI prominence as compared to its pure-play AI SaaS rivals, as they gain traction (users and revenue), while broadening their monetization levers across with its multi-modal creations. And with content creation moving into the agentic future, I believe such threats (and the relatively languid AI revenue accretion) are expected to persist for Adobe investors.

In my previous ADBE article, I had predicated my optimism on the company's ability to overcome the menaces from the Gen AI companies. These companies have continued to gain from strength to strength. Yet, Adobe hasn't quite garnered sufficient traction on its AI ARR to suggest that the company has credibly fended off the disruption risks. In addition, some of these AI leaders have seen their valuation surge through the roof, while ADBE's valuation undergoes a further digestion, with the stock falling to lows last seen in May 2023 recently.

Thus, I believe SaaS investors are finding it increasingly challenging to justify ADBE's historical valuation multiples when assessing whether the current entry points are opportune in catching the falling knife. While Adobe has attempted to lift the market's pessimism by raising its guidance recently, I believe it's only fair that the market is looking for the "hockey stick inflection point" on its AI revenue to validate its ability to keep pace with the leading rivals in the AI market.

And the fact that ADBE's market cap of less than $170B is far below the $300B in valuation of OpenAI based on the most recent funding round, the market is looking for proof points that show that Adobe isn't "bleeding out" from AI disruption.

Adobe: High-Flying Days Are Well Over

Given that the image and video creation tools of Adobe's rivals has become increasingly more realistic, Adobe faces a true market reckoning. In addition, it also faces significant headwinds in the document cloud segment, as even marketing automation SaaS rivals like Salesforce (CRM), are also facing risks from AI disruption of their own. While Adobe has maintained resilient margins over its products (mitigating imminent risks of a fallout), the days of a fast-growing Adobe is conceivably well over.

Coupled with the lack of robust AI revenue growth, the market will likely impose a more substantive discount against the risks imputed to the slew of burgeoning Gen AI leaders. Hence, I think Adobe has its work cut out as it navigates tepid growth momentum. While its solid margins and sticky subscription revenue base has helped to temper increased pessimism on the stock, questions about whether it's enough to help overcome the bearish bias on the stock is expected to linger.

Buy Or Sell ADBE Stock?

In contrast to the strongly bullish IGV ETF, ADBE has fallen profoundly into a bearish downtrend, as seen above. The series of lower-highs and lower-lows price structures corroborate my analysis, raising the specter of a persistent bearish bias that could stymie a decisive bullish reversal at the current levels.

While the stock has managed to recover from its recent May 2025 lows, even the near-term revenue and earnings upgrades on ADBE by Wall Street has failed to spur a further recovery.

ADBE's forward EBITDA multiple of 13.8x is close to the -2 standard deviation zone under its 10Y average. In addition, it's also markedly below the software median of 21.5x, suggesting its valuation shouldn't be construed as aggressive.

However, I also recognize that while ABDE is seemingly "cheap," its fundamental factors that previously underpinned its bullish thesis has clearly been upended by the Gen AI competitors. And unless Adobe claws itself out of the quagmire and improve its AI ARR substantially, the specter of slowing growth in the medium term can no longer be downplayed.

Investors should consider companies with a much more robust AI revenue exposure as the leading proponent in this AI arms race, and Adobe clearly doesn't look like it has closed the gap enough to convince me of its AI pedigree.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: Oscar Health Could Be The Next Growth Monster With Tremendous Upside
data.description:
It's been a wild year for growth stocks, as it seems like this has been the market's focus again. Shares of Palantir (PLTR) have run 490.46% over the past year, as it's now a $324.25 billion company. Robinhood (HOOD) has participated in the rally as its share price has increased 211.45% over the past year, while Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) has increased 127.66% over the past year. The market has been rewarding companies with strong business models that can deliver profitability while maintaining strong growth rates. The next one that could participate in the growth rally is Oscar Health (NYSE:OSCR). OSCR is trying to redefine the health insurance landscape through its technology-enabled vision. OSCR could become a large player in the space, as the healthcare industry is ripe for disruption through innovative products. While other insurance companies have been built on legacy platforms, OSCR is harnessing AI and data analytics to make technology its foundational edge. OSCR is delivering member-centric tools, which include an intuitive mobile app and 24/7 telemedicine, to drive customer satisfaction and retention. There are certainly risks to OSCR that PLTR, HOOD, and HIMS don't face, but if OSCR can deliver on its innovative platform and become a new standard for healthcare delivery by personalizing care, it could outpace its legacy competitors and deliver tremendous upside for investors who are willing to make unconventional investments that have more risk associated with them.

Risks to investing in Oscar Health

While I think that OSCR presents an interesting opportunity to invest in a healthcare insurance platform before it turns into a large provider, there are risks that investors should consider. Everyone should do their own due diligence as this is a complex industry. OSCR was founded in 2012 and is a technology-driven health insurance company. OSCR relies heavily on insurance premiums for revenue, but managing claims and controlling costs haven't been easy. Since going public, OSCR has only had 1 profitable year, which was 2024, as it generated $25.4 million in net income. Only 1 quarter in 2025 has been reported, and the trailing twelve-month (TTM) net income is $123.3 million, which isn't a lot of breathing room. The health insurance market is dominated by established giants like UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and Cigna (CI). UNH and CI benefit from extensive provider networks and economies of scale, and OSCR's tech-driven model may not be enough to disrupt these incumbents. OSCR's growth has been tied to the Affordable Care Act (ACA) which expanded access to individual health plans, and any changes in healthcare policy could disrupt its business model. Depending on what a Republican-led government gets passed through the legislative branches, it could reduce ACA subsidies or alter regulations, which could negatively impact OSCR's revenue base and business operations. Investors should do their own research because OSCR comes with a lot of different risk factors, which is the trade-off for massive growth potential.

Oscar Health has a strong set of financials with massive growth on the horizon if the analyst community is correct

When I went through OSCR's history as a public company, their financials stood out, as I believe there is a lot of value to be unlocked. OSCR is an insurance company, so one of the main aspects to look at is the amount of revenue they're taking in from their premiums and annuities compared to what their policy benefits are. Insurance companies typically have a large expense on the income statement because the policy benefits refer to the costs associated with paying out claims and providing healthcare services to policyholders. When I look at the data on a quarterly basis, it gets me bullish on their potential. Over the past 10 quarters, the premium and annuity revenue that OSCR has generated increased by 212% ($2.04 billion) while its policy benefits increased by 165.01% ($1.41 billion). This is a strong spread where the revenue growth from its policies is outpacing the claims OSCR is paying out. Their margins are also improving, as their policy benefit to premium revenue was 88.8% in Q3 2022 and has dropped to 75.43% in Q1 2025. Looking at the Q1 metrics, OSCR's premium and annuity revenue increased by 27.74% QoQ to $3 billion ($650.60 billion), while its policy benefits only increased 9.42% ($194.60 million) to $2.26 billion.

OSCR is becoming increasingly profitable and is utilizing its cash flow to benefit shareholders. OSCR has a strong balance sheet, with $2.24 billion in cash and $2.62 billion in total investments. After OSCR's liabilities are accounted for between unpaid claims, and the insurance and annuity liabilities, OSCR still has $1.44 billion on the balance sheet between investments and cash. There is only $299.7 million in long-term debt, and OSCR has a low share count of 254.4 million. Over the past year, OSCR has generated $10.08 billion in revenue and produced $1.19 billion in free cash flow (FCF) and $123.40 in net income. In Q1, OSCR generated $3.05 billion in revenue, which was an increase of 42.20% YoY. From a profitability perspective, OSCR produced $869.50 in FCF in Q1, which was an increase of 38.37% ($241.10 million) YoY, while its net income grew 55.19% ($97.90 million) YoY to $275.30 million. OSCR isn't issuing new debt, and in Q1, they purchased $900,000 worth of shares as they implemented their first quarter of share repurchases. I'm seeing a lot to like in the financials, and OSCR is in a position where it can continue to increase its profitability while having its current liabilities covered by its on-hand liquidity from the balance sheet.

The analyst community is expecting tremendous EPS growth from OSCR over the next 2 fiscal years as they see profitability expanding. OSCR is expected to generate $0.68 in EPS during 2025 and $1.96 of EPS in 2027. OSCR is currently trading at 20.51 times 2025 earnings, and with 188.24% EPS growth on the horizon over the next 2 fiscal years, it's trading at 7.12 times 2027 earnings. When I look at other competitors in the field, including Unum Group (UNM), MetLife (MET), Aflac (AFL), Humana (HUM), and UNH, OSCR looks undervalued. While OSCR hasn't established a long-term track record like its peers, if the analyst community is correct, OSCR could be a steal at these levels as you are getting a tremendous amount of growth at an inexpensive price.

Oscar Health is building an innovative insurance platform that can disrupt its competitors

OSCR's mission is to make a healthier life accessible and affordable for all, and it was established with the idea to challenge the status quo in the healthcare system. OSCR sells insurance to individuals, families, and employees through federal and state-run healthcare exchanges established under the ACA. OSCR is leveraging its proprietary technology platform known as +Oscar to provide health technology solutions to create a better platform for its members. The way that OSCR differentiates itself from traditional health insurers is through its technology-centric approach, providing a superior member experience to drive engagement and create operational efficiencies. OSCR has built a full-stack technology platform, which is integral to delivering its goals. +Oscar provides a free live chat feature for Oscar Virtual Urgent Care, which gathers patient symptoms and severity before provider engagement, reducing member response times by 90% and boosting provider efficiency by 28%. It also has an embedded AI tool designed to help care guides more quickly address member needs. OSCR has positioned itself as a modern healthcare company that uses technology and a comprehensive approach to member well-being to distinguish its insurance offerings and services.

At the end of Q1, OSCR's membership reached approximately 2 million members, which represents a 41% increase YoY. The retention and new membership growth achieved by OSCR reflect the value of its innovative plan designs and superior member experience. Their individual and small group membership totaled 2,021,484 members at the end of Q1, compared to 1,386,980 at the end of Q1 2024, representing an increase of 45.75%. OSCR's tech stack is instrumental in achieving fixed cost leverage and variable cost efficiencies, which has resulted in the lowest Selling, General & Administrative (SG&A) expense ratio in their history, which came in at 15.8% in Q1 2025. OSCR continues to expand its market presence through new partnerships that provide members with value-added services, such as Oscar Community Resources with Find Help, a social care network. This program connects members with essential local services (e.g., food, housing, transportation) that extend "beyond medical care that impacts health," which they believe will support clinical intervention and case management programs to lower spend, and improve clinical outcomes.

Conclusion

The market may still view OSCR as speculative, but it's turned the corner on profitability and has a strong set of financials. OSCR has been successful at scaling its membership while utilizing its core technology as a differentiator for efficiency and member engagement. OSCR continues to deepen its market presence through new partnerships, and its membership reached 2 million in Q1 while generating $3 billion in revenue. The gap between premiums and annuity revenue, compared to policy benefits, continues to expand, as policy benefits now represent 75.43% of the revenue generated from these policies. OSCR has a strong balance sheet, with 63% of its market capitalization held in cash and $1.44 billion in investments and cash, after accounting for OSCR's liabilities, including unpaid claims and insurance and annuity liabilities. While there are risks associated with potential changes to state-issued programs under the ACA in future regulations, the analyst community anticipates significant future growth for OSCR, and the risk-reward balance is very interesting, with the market cap hovering around the $3 billion level.

data.title: Iamgold: Stock's Success Is Likely To Continue
data.description:
IAMGOLD (NYSE:IAG) is poised to continue its success as the company increases production. The stock's valuation is still a bargain, leaving plenty of room to the upside. The strong price of gold should provide a tailwind for the company.

The company performed well so far this year since my last IAG article from January 2025. I pointed out then that IAG's strong growth and low valuation was likely to drive the stock for significant gains. The stock increased 22% since then. I expect further gains in 2025 as the company continues its strong production.

IAMGOLD's Growth Catalysts

IAMGOLD achieved strong results in Q1 2025. This sets the company up well for future gains this year and beyond. IAG's total production increased 6.6% in Q1 2025 to 161,000 ounces from its three mines. The company achieved revenue of $477 million from sales of 174,000 ounces at an average gold price of $2,731/oz. Q1 2025 revenue was 41% higher than Q1 2024.

IAG's adjusted EBITDA increased 34% to $204.5 million in Q1. Total mine site free cash flow increased 204% to $140 million. The large increase in mine site free cash flow was due to the Cote mine contribution of $57.6 million which didn't have any reportable cash flow for Q1 2024. It was also a result of the Essakane mine's 83% increase in mine site free cash flow to $65.4 million and Westwood's 58% increase to $16.6 million.

The Cote mine is a large contributor for IAG's growth as it produced 1 million tonnes in March & April of 2025. The Cote mine is now operating at 90% of capacity. IAG expects Cote to produce 36,000 tonnes per day by the end of the year as production capacity continues to increase.

IAG expects to achieve improvements in grade reconciliation and stronger quality gold production for the remainder of 2025 at its Essakane mine. The company is focusing on expanding its underground mining areas and to continue to its strong record of resource to reserve conversion at the Westwood mine.

Overall, IAG expects production of 735,000 to 820,000 ounces in 2025. This range would be 10% to 23% above 2024's production of 667,000 ounces. The company plans on consistently achieving annual production of 800,000 for the future. This ramp up over time should help drive IAMGOLD's long-term growth.

Here's a breakdown of IAG's expected production from the recent 6-F report:

Fitch recently reaffirmed IAG's long-term issuer default rating at B+ (outlook stable). This is the same rating that competitor, Eldorado Gold (EGO), received from Fitch. Fitch cited that IAMGOLD improved its operational profile by now owning 70% of the Cote Gold project. Fitch also stated that the Cote mine will increase IAG's annual production by about 300,000 ounces from 2025 to 2028. Fitch also estimated IAG's gold price sensitivity that a $100 drop in the price of gold would reduce EBITDA by about $74 million in 2025. That was assuming an average price of gold of $2,400 per ounce this year. So, IAG is in good shape so far with the price of gold above that level up to now in 2025.

IAG's balance sheet shows strength with 1.4x more current assets than current liabilities and 2.8x more total assets than total liabilities for total equity of $3.4 billion. The company has $317.3 million in total cash/equivalents and $819.1 million in net debt. The cash position increased by about 9% year-over-year while the net debt increased by nearly 28%. There is some room for improvement regarding cash growth vs. net debt growth. However, gold production is a capital intensive business, so having a high amount of debt can be common in the industry.

With a high amount of assets and positive operating cash flow, IAMGOLD looks to be in good shape to handle its long-term debt. The company did repay $67.5 million in long-term debt in Q1 2025 which was higher than the $50 million that was issued.

Valuation

IAMGOLD is trading at 10x expected EPS of $0.74 for 2025. This is lower than the Gold industry's forward PE of 13.7x. However, it is a bit higher than IAG's forward PE of 7.2x from when I last wrote about the company in January 2025. Eldorado Gold is trading with a higher forward PE of 13.7x expected EPS of $1.57 for 2025.

With IAG still trading below the industry, I see the valuation as attractive. This undervaluation still provides room for PE expansion as IAG continues to grow.

I will point out that IAG's EPS estimate of $0.74 is a few points lower than the $0.79 that was estimated back in January. However, it is probably not enough to cause negative sentiment for the stock unless more significant downward revisions are made.

The current EPS estimate of $0.74 represents 44% earnings growth. That is higher than the Gold industry's expected annual growth of 21% over the next five years. While IAG's growth looks high, it can be reasonably achieved with IAG's ramp up of production. The increased production and the strong price of gold is expected to drive a 49% increase in revenue in 2025 (consensus). The high revenue growth and IAG's ROE of 28% and ROA of 15% can help the company hit the EPS estimate.

Technical Perspective

IAMGOLD's monthly chart above shows the stock's strong uptrend since the end of 2022. The purple RSI line (middle of the chart) did move into the overbought territory above 70. However, the stock has been chopping around the overbought level on the monthly chart since October 2024. The stock has been going higher during this time. So, I wouldn't be too worried about the overbought condition unless the RSI moves past 80. It is currently in the mid-70s.

The MACD indicator (bottom of the chart) is also positive as the blue line remains above the red signal line and with the histogram bars in the green.

Continued positive production and earnings news from IAG as the year continues could keep the stock going higher. Investors should reasonably expect the occasional pullback along the way on profit taking.

The Risks for IAMGOLD

IAMGOLD depends on the price of gold which helps drive its revenue per ounce sold. A significant drop in the price of gold would lead to IAG to earn less in revenue and EBITDA.

The company is subject to government regulations in the countries where it operates. For example, IAG pays Burkina Faso royalties on the gold that the company mines for the Essakane mine. It is possible that the royalty rates could be increased in the future, which could lower IAG's earnings. Other regulations from these governments could make doing business more expensive for IAG.

The nature of gold mining is capital intensive. IAG tends to run with a high amount of debt in relation to cash. If conditions for gold mining and the company's cash flow deteriorated significantly, IAMGOLD could be challenged to pay off its debt.

IAMGOLD's Long-Term Outlook

IAMGOLD looks poised for long-term growth. The strong price of gold above $3,000 per ounce is positive for the company to maximize revenue and earnings. IAG's efforts to increase production and mine capacity should drive strong long-term growth over multiple years.

IAG's low valuation leaves room for multiple expansion as the company increases earnings. IAG's covering analysts have a one-year price target of $8.44 for the stock. This would be 10.6% above the current price. This looks too conservative to me. I think that the stock can hit $10 within a year for a 31% gain.

The target of $10 would bring the PE to 13.5x expected EPS of $0.74 for 2025. This is near the industry PE average. Since IAG is growing revenue and earnings at an above-average pace, investors are likely to bid up the stock price at a strong pace.

data.title: Empire State Realty Trust: I'm Not A Buyer At Today's Prices
data.description:
Empire State Realty Trust (NYSE:ESRT) is a fairly unique REIT. It is an almost pure play on the New York City commercial real estate market, and it also owns a trophy tourism asset.

As the name would suggest, this REIT owns the Empire State Building along with a number of office properties and 732 residential units in the New York City metro area. It also generates significant tourism revenues from its observation deck at the Empire State Building along with collecting rents from retail stores located in the ground floors of its various properties.

I had previously owned Empire State Realty Trust shares prior to the pandemic believing that the observation deck was an underappreciated asset that should enjoy considerable growth in the years to come. Unfortunately, the pandemic completely derailed my thesis disrupting both the tourism business and the core NYC office leasing market. I ultimately realized my loss and sold the position.

The stock has continued to underperform in recent years:

With that context in mind, let's take a fresh look at Empire State Realty Trust today.

The Market's Skepticism Makes Sense

To be frank, I don't see considerable value even after the recent underperformance. I do not expect ESRT stock to considerably outperform either the REIT indexes or the S&P 500 going forward. Let me explain way.

First off, it's important to realize that there are different tiers of buildings and assets within the office market. And there are decidedly different forward outlooks depending on the type of office assets that you own right now.

I do think we've seen the peak, or at least the near-term peak, in the remote and hybrid work models. A lot of companies, even including tech firms, are increasingly returning to the office. I expect that trend to continue. And with the job market getting tougher, this puts more bargaining power in the hands of employers. Previously, if a company demanded return to the office, a worker could simply hop to any of the other firms in their field that were still hiring remote workers. With the job market turning south, however, workers will increasingly have to comply with return to the office mandates or else face unemployment.

All that to say that there should be some incremental demand for office space in general and New York City should be no exception to the broader trend. And NYC should be a better market than many U.S. cities due to its large size and economic vitality. NYC has exposure to a lot of high-end industries that have more sustained job growth possibilities. And if office rents do come down, that should unlock more demand from firms that were previously priced out of the NYC office market and want to have a physical presence in that key economic hub.

In short, I expect New York City will continue to be able to attract additional office clients even as smaller or less prosperous U.S. cities see sharp declines in office leasing and pricing.

That said, it seems evident that the office rental market has changed since pre-pandemic conditions. I doubt that we'll ever see the office market return to the robust levels of activity and valuation that we saw 20 or 25 years ago.

As this relates to Empire State Realty in particular, the key issue is that the company's buildings are quite old. Most of its properties were built many decades ago. That includes the landmark Empire State Building which opened its doors during the 1930s.

As we start to approach the 2030s, it's increasingly difficult for these much older office buildings to adapt. They were not built to today's architectural standards and face challenges in terms of modernizing them to meet current client demand. It requires a significant deal of CAPEX to reposition older buildings so that they remain competitive against newer structures. In addition, overall maintenance costs on a property tend to rise as a skyscraper ages.

Older buildings also have a bigger challenge in terms of trying to potentially convert them from office space to other uses such as apartments. It's my understanding that older NYC skyscrapers would, in most cases, not be profitable targets for potential conversion into residential use.

Not The Right Office REIT For The Current Market

I'm not opposed to the concept of investing in offices in top tier U.S. cities at the right price. There is still money to be made in some select office markets.

I'd be more concerned, however, if my core investment thesis was built on the premise that a collection of older buildings was going to outcompete much newer office properties. I don't see that as an especially likely outcome.

Empire State Realty still has its two other key revenue streams that could offset its weaker positioning in core office leasing.

However, I see both retail and tourism potentially having issues in part due to the soft overall economy and also due to public perception issues. On the tourism front, a recent New York Times article noted that the city's tourism agency cut its 2025 tourist forecast once again, driven by a projected 800,000 person decline in international tourist arrivals this year.

In addition to a slide in international tourism potentially linked to concerns over tariffs and immigration policy, public safety issues may be staining NYC's image. In December, The Atlantic argued that New York City has lost control of crime, pointing to a surge in violent attacks on the subway system. A recent Bloomberg editorial pointed out that while NYC's violent crime rate has dipped a bit in 2025, the city's violent crime rate remains about 32% above pre-pandemic levels. That's a notable contrast to the nation as a whole, where violent crime levels are now lower than pre-pandemic figures.

I would note that New York City has a mayoral election coming up and a self-described socialist candidate, Zohran Mamdani, has been surging in the polls. If he does win and enacts his campaign promises such as rent freezes and government-run grocery stores, this could have a negative impact on NYC's perceived business climate and cause some firms to forego renting offices in the city in the future.

Dividend Yield Is Inadequate For Today's Market Conditions

My final issue with this REIT today is that it yields just 1.6%. The firm suspended its dividend at the onset of the pandemic. The dividend returned at a far lower level in 2021 and has not been increased subsequently:

In a time when you can earn almost 5% on risk-free U.S. government bonds, I find a 1.6% yield from a REIT fairly uninspiring. You could make the case for owning a low-yielder if the REIT was growing quickly and would see sharply higher cash flows in the future. However, in the case of Empire State Realty, I don't see significant growth going forward meaning that there is not an obvious benefit in return for the low dividend yield today.

It's possible that the company could turn its properties around and generate more cash flows going forward. That said, occupancy seems likely to remain around 90% on the company's buildings in coming quarters while rents seem fairly unlikely to grow more quickly than inflation.

Put another way, I believe this will remain a structurally challenged portfolio. Given the age of the buildings and the difficulties for the office market in general, I simply don't see much upside here beyond rents simply keeping up with inflation. And the starting valuation isn't low enough to make that future return profile particularly attractive. Additionally, the low dividend yield is a distinct negative for a REIT in the current interest rate environment.

And on the downside, there is material risk here if New York City's business conditions or tourism appeal weaken. I'm also concerned for retail leasing given the challenges that retail is facing at the moment.

I still believe that the Empire State Building's observatory is a unique asset. It's something I would like to own if it were available at the right price. However, ESRT shares are still trading at around 10x price-to-funds from operations (P/FFO). That doesn't seem like a particular bargain given that several other U.S. office REITs are trading at a single-digit P/FFO ratio.

data.title: Michelin: Looking At The Upside For 2025-2028
data.description:
If you're looking to make a nice 15-18% annualized rate of return with considerable safety, it's my view that the European market and some of its undervalued companies are the way to go - and I would point to my own investments over the past year as examples of such returns.

Many of the companies In the European sector that I have invested in have, over the past few months, already realized significant upside. These companies include mostly financial stocks such as AXA (OTCQX:AXAHY) and Societe Generale (OTCPK:SCGLY), but also include industrial and construction companies such as Vinci (OTCPK:VCISY). All of these investments have returned in excess of 35-80% RoR for the time I've held them, and many I have already rotated, turning a "paper profit" into realized profits.
