I seem to be one of the primary analysts covering the Central European, and particularly the French market, at this time on Seeking Alpha. It's a market that I have come more heavily into over the past 3 years time or so, and one I believe will have significant upside in the coming years.

Unlike many dividend-oriented investors, my focus has become on both the buying, the holding, and the selling of stocks, in combination with dividend-aware investing. I have found that this seems to work very well for my approach.

In today's article, I will be looking at Michelin (OTCPK:MGDDY)(OTCPK:MGDDF). Despite their products being well-known across the world, the coverage on Seeking Alpha as well as other outlets is rather modest given what the company represents. I mean to change this, at least here, by updating and giving my take on Michelin.

And, as a preview, it's a positive take with a "BUY" rating at this particular time, after Q1'25.

Let me show you what the reason for this is.

Michelin - the volatility of tires with the upside of quality and fundamentals

Michelin, like Continental (OTCPK:CTTAY), is a tricky investment. I don't particularly like the business models of tire companies, so I tend to invest in them only when they are very cheap. It's a heavily commoditized business, with the exception of certain sub-segments and customers buying quality tires above commodity ones. Perhaps part of my interest has to do with the fact that I am one of the investors who tend to buy "good" tires, usually Michelin or Continental, for my vehicles. Where I live, the change between -30 degrees and +25-30 degrees Celsius means that you want good tires, not only for the winter when it's icy, but also for the summer, when rain can make the surface equally slippery. I've never seen the point in "skimping" on the part of the vehicle that has constant contact with the road.

That's my interest in Michelin and what it does. When we look at company trends, in terms of volume growth, income, and profit, however, we quickly see the volatility that is part of the business model.

However, against the trend and the volatility you see above is actually the fact that the company has, over time, reliably with only a few exceptions, generated cash of over €1.7B on an annual basis, and couples this with a gearing of less than 17% at the end of the 2024 fiscal. This has been recognized by S&P, by Fitch, by Scope, and by Moody's, which all rate the company A. And this is part of what makes the company attractive.

Aside from this appeal, the company has a diverse sales and production base. While not completely insulated from the impacts of tariffs, I don't see a major problem or issue for the business, no matter where this goes.

Aside from this, the company is seeing the same trends that other tire companies are seeing for the macro - meaning that trucks are generally up, especially in global market, and replacement markets remain resilient, with OE markets continuing to be problematic, down 6% in trucks and about 1% in LVP's. The company has exposure to specialty solutions and products, including things like Aircraft tires, mining tires, and beyond-road tires (tractors and the like), and most of these markets are going fairly well also.

In the OE sector, the company remains a major partner for most big manufacturers, and this also includes supercar/luxury car manufacturers. The company also partners with world leaders in braking systems.

Michelin doesn't just do aircraft tires, but also things like composite solutions for planes, which includes sealing applications for cargo doors, for engine gearbox fire detection, for control components - in short, the company also has workings/exposure to what is certainly non-commoditized segments, which also includes 100% PFAS free solutions for the industry - a major advantage in the current macro going forward.

Its industrial production base is mixed. It has production in the USA, Germany, France, Poland, and China, among other things. It's currently in the process of addressing its production base and increasing productivity using AI and digital, which is expected to drive savings.

Where other tire manufacturers seem focused on improving their base operations, Michelin couples this with exposure to non-commoditized sectors to offset cyclicality. Against cyclicality, the company is increasingly working with indexation clauses for OE contracts, but this still cannot offset the negative volume growth currently affecting the industry - negative 7.3% against a 4.8% positive price mix, and about 0.7% positive currency FC, leading to around a €100M drop in net sales for the Q1'25 period, the latest quarter that we have to look at here.

The company remains attractive to its shareholders. Michelin pays a dividend of over 4% in yield, and has maintained this for 2024/2025. The current level is at a 2.2% growth versus last, and they couple this with a €1B share buyback program is likely to support the level of share valuation here.

While the market and the general trends are seeing challenges and issues, the company is on track when it comes to its overall goals.

This is further confirmed by increases in operating income, as well as operating margin. In 2017, Michelin managed an 11-12% operating margin, which is now up over 13% and targeting 14% for 2026E due to efficiencies. Include also that operating income has improved from €2.8B back in 2017, to an estimated €4.2B in 2026, and you can understand that there's a significant evolution of the upside here. FCF is expected to improve from €4B back in 2017-2019 to a cumulative level of €5.5B in 2024-2026E, and increase well over 10%.

While this is ongoing, the company has not broached the 50% payout level, and remains below this, while also increasing its ROCE by over 90 bps in less than 3-5 years. This tells me that company management is executing on a good strategy, and this strategy is giving results - which will likely be rewarded by the market eventually (and if it is not being rewarded, that's okay too - we'll simply continue to invest at a high yield/upside until it does, just like with other companies that I invest in.)

In short, plenty to like here. Unfortunately, if you're investing now, you're not really getting the company at a super-cheap valuation, but an okay one with a good upside. That's what we'll go into in the valuation section - to see what sort of upside the company gives us at a native share price of just around €32/share.

Michelin - Upside at €32/share is good enough for a significant return.

So, if you follow my work, you know my approach - I use valuation methods (DCF, NAV multiples, estimated EPS growth), combine this with historical forecast accuracy as well as peer multiples to establish what I would consider a fair value for the company in question. I then look at that fair value and ask what sort of price would give me a 15% annualized upside, or above, and that's where my price target (PT) comes for the company in question. Other analysts sometimes work with just fair values as their price target - given that I have a "hurdle" which I am trying to beat, I don't consider this to be good enough for me.

Michelin's native ticker in Paris is called ML - the ADR I will give a PT for later. ML trades at a current average P/E of around 11x, averaging 3 years in forecast and past. The company has an average EPS growth rate of around 6% per year for the past 20 years. The yield is 4.32%. Historically, we have a 5-year valuation average of around 12.24x, implying that a 10- 11x P/E is below this, and a 20-year average of 12.34x, almost identical. It therefore seems fair to say that 12- 13x P/E is what you can expect from Michelin, which makes 10- 11x potentially attractive.

The company hits about 60-70% of the time on a 2-year basis, beats a few times, misses about 25-30% of the time. A bit mixed, but nothing to greatly impair an already discounted 12-13x P/E. I don't see any reason based on peers, such as Nokian or Continental, to discount this much further. Both of these companies are tire companies I also invest in, for mixed reasons.

Assuming a 7-11% growth rate, which is not unrealistic based on the current efficiency forecasts, a 12.3x forward P/E would come to a fair value of €50/share for 2027E. However, given the misses we're seeing, and given that the EPS growth rate is outside of this company's historical likelihood, I am discounting the company's fair value by 10% at this time, to around €45/share. That means in order to get a 15% annualized upside to a €45/share, you'd need to invest at €34.5/share or below. The current trading level for ML is €32.66/share as I am writing this.

This implies an upside of about 16.6% annualized, or just north of 60% in 3 years, inclusive of dividends for the 2027E period.

If investing at this level, I believe the downside is sufficiently protected, and even in the case of worse-than-expected returns, you need not suffer too large declines in the short term, coupled with the dividend.

Risks to the thesis

The risks for Michelin are centered around the notoriously and historically proven volatile industry of tire manufacturing - one I am too familiar with after investing in both Continental and Nokian, as peers to Michelin. Companies in this segment, in geopolitical risk-on situations, can go negative - and Michelin has in the past as well. This doesn't mean that the company or the shares necessarily crash, but the company's dividend usually goes to zero in those situations.

So the risk is that in a situation where we see further downside, investors in Tire companies aren't all that protected (same goes for Continental). The right time to buy them is at incredibly cheap levels. While Michelin is cheap enough, it's not exactly "incredibly cheap" - it's an okay valuation with an upside. Therefore, I view one of the primary risks for this investment as the valuation, which might be low, but perhaps not low enough for a scenario where we see further pressure.

But given that I see the likelihood of this as low, I consider the risk/reward ratio here as favorable enough to justify an investment.

It means that as things stand now, I am prepared to give Michelin a positive "BUY" with a PT of €34.5/share. For the ADR MGDDY this comes to a translated PT of $20/share.

Thesis

  • Michelin is one of the world's leading manufacturers of quality/premium tires, as well as products for the aerospace industry, with an attractive sales mix and an attractively optimized production base. The company, despite this, is extremely well leveraged at below 18%, A-rated, and with a 4 %+ yield that's based on a sub-50% EPS payout ratio. This makes it, at the right valuation, a very investable business.
  • I would value Michelin at a conservative forward P/E of around 12.5x, which with a conservative growth rate comes to a fair value of €45/share, and a 15%+ annualized upside from a PT of €34.5/share.
  • This means that as of June 2025, with the company trading at around €32.60/share, the company represents a "BUY" with an upside of over 15% per annum until 2027E, based on very conservative estimates. It's therefore an attractive investment, and one I consider good enough to "BUY" for your investment portfolio, if your risk considerations and hurdles align with what is offered here.
  • I am now LONG Michelin with an expected holding period of 3-5 years, depending on when the company

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall high quality.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €34.5/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of June 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
